title: The Dream That Burns
prompt: A dark, expansive shot of a windswept cliff edge at twilight, where Orin, a lean and tall human shaman with deep brown skin, a shaved head marked by ritual scars along his temples, and deep-set eyes that faintly glow with amber prophetic light, kneels with his gnarled staff planted before him. He wears layered animal hides adorned with bone talismans that clink in the wind. His eyes are rolled back, caught in a waking vision. Behind him, the sky churns with unnatural violet clouds shaped like reaching claws. The color palette is bruised purple and sickly amber. The camera angle is low, shooting upward against Orin's silhouette, emphasizing the vast hostile sky. Wisps of dark smoke curl from the cracks in the earth around his knees.
narrative: Orin stands at the cliff's edge, gripped by a vision he cannot control. A darkness is coming — not from the sky, but from beneath the soil itself. He sees bones rising, ancient things reassembling. And in the vision's final breath, he sees a face he does not recognize: broad-browed, fierce, painted in ash.
narration: Orin does not choose his visions. They choose him. They arrive like wolves — silent, then sudden, sinking teeth into the soft place behind his eyes. Tonight the vision is different. It smells of old earth and iron. Something is waking beneath the frozen ground. Something that does not distinguish between his people and others. He sees bones knitting together in the dark. He sees fire. He sees a face — not human, but undeniably alive. Painted in ash. Watching him from the other side of a chasm that has no bottom. When the vision releases him, his nose is bleeding, and the wind has gone still. Far too still.

title: The Ash-Marked Seer
prompt: Deep inside a torch-lit cave, Azra, a stocky and powerfully built Neanderthal woman with a prominent brow ridge, wide nose, deep-set amber eyes, and thick auburn hair bound back with braided leather cords, sits cross-legged before a ring of carved bone totems. She wears heavy fur wraps over broad shoulders, and her face is streaked with ash and ochre in sacred geometric patterns. Stone pendants carved with animal figures hang at her chest. Her large, calloused hands hover over a shallow stone basin filled with dark water that reflects no torchlight. The camera is positioned low and to the side, capturing her profile against the flickering fire. The palette is warm amber and deep shadow, with the water basin emitting an eerie blue-black glow at center. Her expression is tense — jaw set, nostrils flared.
narrative: Azra reads the water as her elders taught her, but what she sees tonight makes her pull her hands back as if burned. The water shows her bones — her people's burial grounds — cracking open from below. And at the edge of her vision, a figure stands watching. A stranger. Human.
narration: The water speaks to Azra the way stone speaks to river — slowly, then all at once. She has read the signs since she was a child, hands steady, heart unshaken. But tonight the water turns black and will not show the sky. It shows her the bone-fields where her ancestors sleep. She watches the earth split. She watches shapes rise that should not rise. And standing at the rim of ruin, she sees a man. Thin. Scarred. Human. He is looking straight at her, as though the water is his mirror too. Azra pulls her hands from the basin. Her fingers tremble — not from cold. From something she has not felt in a long time. Recognition. And dread.

title: The Trail of Broken Signs
prompt: A wide cinematic shot of a desolate frozen tundra at dawn, where Orin walks alone along a narrow trail between jagged black rocks. His lean frame is hunched against a cutting wind, his layered hides snapping behind him. His staff glows faintly at its amber tip, lighting the frozen ground in a small cone of warm amber. Scattered along the trail are strange signs — claw marks in the stone that glow with a faint sickly green phosphorescence. The camera is positioned at a high three-quarter angle, showing Orin small against the enormous, hostile landscape. The color palette is ashen grey, ice blue, and the toxic green of the claw marks. Storm clouds press low overhead. In the far distance, barely visible, smoke rises from a settlement nestled against a mountainside.
narrative: Orin follows the signs his visions laid before him — claw marks in living stone, corrupted earth, a sickness spreading beneath the world. The trail leads him toward the Neanderthal highlands, toward the face from his vision. He approaches with no weapons drawn. Only a warning.
narration: Three days he has walked. Three days since the vision burned itself behind his eyes and set his feet in motion. The marks in the stone are deeper now — clawed things dragging themselves through solid rock. Whatever is waking, it is closer here. Orin's staff hums against his palm, feeding him fragments: a cave mouth, the smell of ochre, the sound of drums he does not recognize. He knows what waits ahead. Not an ally. Not yet. Only a stranger painted in ash, whose people have every reason to drive a spear through a human shaman's ribs. He adjusts his grip on the staff. Keeps walking. The smoke rises closer now.

title: Spears at the Threshold
prompt: A tense medium-wide shot at the mouth of a narrow mountain pass. Azra stands at the front of a group of five Neanderthal warriors, each broad and heavily furred in thick pelts, holding crude but deadly stone-tipped spears angled forward. Azra's amber eyes are narrowed with suspicion, her jaw clenched, one hand raised in a halting gesture. Facing her, ten paces away, Orin stands with his staff held horizontally across his chest in a gesture of peace, palms open and visible. His posture is straight but careful, his ritual scars catching the dim overcast light. The camera is positioned between them at ground level, creating a confrontational composition with the two figures flanking the frame's edges, the narrow rocky pass between them like a canyon of tension. The palette is cold steel grey for the stone, warm ochre and amber for the Neanderthals' furs and paint, and the pale grey-blue of Orin's exposed hands.
narrative: Azra blocks the pass. She has seen this man in the water. She does not trust what she has seen. Orin raises no weapon. He speaks. She does not answer. The spears do not lower.
narration: She is exactly as the water showed her. Broad. Fierce. Eyes like banked fire. Orin stops walking. He holds his staff across his chest and lets the silence speak for him. The warriors behind Azra shift — restless, ready. A single word from her would end this. She does not give it. Instead she studies him the way one studies a wound: carefully, with disgust, and with the knowledge that ignoring it could kill. Seconds stretch into something heavier. Orin does not move. He has seen what is coming. He knows that what stands between them is smaller than what stands behind them both. But knowing that and proving it are different things. The wind cuts through the pass. No one flinches.

title: Shared Visions, Shared Dread
prompt: The interior of Azra's torch-lit cave, now crowded with tension. Azra sits on one side of the shallow stone basin, Orin on the opposite side, their reflections both visible in the dark water between them. Azra's broad face is lit from below by the basin's cold blue glow, her ash-and-ochre paint stark, her amber eyes wide with reluctant recognition. Orin's lean face mirrors the same light, his ritual scars casting long shadows, his own eyes glowing faintly amber as he enters a trance. Between them, rising from the water, a translucent vision-construct floats — a skeletal figure made of black bone and green fire, towering and formless. Neanderthal observers stand in the cave's shadows, watching with spears half-raised. The camera is positioned directly overhead, looking straight down at the basin, the two seers, and the ghostly apparition between them. The palette is deep cave shadow, amber torchlight, cold blue water-glow, and the sickly green of the bone creature.
narrative: Azra agrees to see what Orin has seen. They place their hands in the water together. The vision explodes outward, shared between them — a bone entity, ancient and hungry, rising from beneath the world's oldest graves. Both seers recoil. Both now understand the same truth.
narration: The water does not lie. That is the one thing both their traditions agree upon. So Orin lowers his hands. Azra lowers hers. And for a moment the basin belongs to neither of them. It belongs to the vision. The thing that rises from the dark water is made of bone and green flame, and it is vast — older than language, older than fire. It has been sleeping beneath the bone-fields for longer than either people have drawn breath. Orin feels the sear of recognition behind his eyes. Azra feels it in her chest. They pull back at the same instant. The cave is silent except for the hiss of the torches. Azra looks at Orin. Really looks at him, for the first time, without the filter of suspicion. What she sees there is not an enemy. It is a mirror.

title: Roots of the Wound
prompt: A dimly lit scene outside the cave at dusk, where Orin and Azra sit on opposite sides of a low stone outcrop, facing away from each other but close enough to speak. Azra is hunched forward, her thick forearms resting on her knees, one hand gripping a carved stone pendant at her chest — her knuckles white. Her expression is hard, layered with old anger. Orin sits with his back against the rock, staff laid across his lap, his head bowed, the amber stone dark. The camera is positioned in profile, framing both figures against a bruised purple sky streaked with the first unnatural green aurora — a sign of the approaching threat. The palette is muted earth tones, muted by the failing light, with the sickly green aurora bleeding into the upper frame. Their body language speaks volumes — two people forced into proximity by necessity, separated by generations of conflict.
narrative: Alone, away from witnesses, the old wound surfaces. Azra speaks of humans who raided her people's sacred sites. Orin speaks of his own elders' cruelty toward the Neanderthals. Neither apologizes easily. But the honesty sits between them like a stone that cannot be unplaced.
narration: Trust is not a river you cross in a single step. It is a wound you tend, night after night, until the scar tissue is strong enough to hold. Azra tells him about the raids — humans who came to the bone-fields not to honor the dead, but to strip them. She tells him without softening the words. Orin listens. He does not interrupt. When it is his turn, he tells her about his own people's cruelties, spoken like a confession to no priest. The silence between their words is thick. Heavy. But it is not empty. Something is growing there. Not friendship. Not yet. Something harder. Respect, maybe. Or the understanding that survival does not require affection. Only the willingness to stand on the same side of the line when the ground splits open.

title: The Ground Breaks
prompt: A dramatic wide shot of the Neanderthal settlement at night, where the earth is splitting apart in long jagged fissures that emit sickly green-white light from below. From the largest fissure, skeletal forms are emerging — twisted bone constructs with hollow eye sockets burning green, crawling and pulling themselves onto the frozen ground. Neanderthal warriors rush toward the fissures with spears and torches, their broad silhouettes dark against the unearthly glow. In the foreground, Orin and Azra stand side by side at the edge of the largest crack, Orin's staff blazing amber and Azra's hands raised with her carved stone pendants glowing a deep ochre-orange. The camera is positioned low and wide, capturing the epic scale of the breach with the two seers as small but resolute figures at center. The palette is dominated by toxic green from the fissures, warm amber and ochre from the seers' magic, and deep black shadow.
narrative: The bone entity's first wave erupts from the earth. The settlement is under siege. Neanderthal warriors fight with brutal efficiency, but the constructs keep coming. Orin and Azra stand at the main fissure, pouring their combined sight into the breach, trying to understand — and slow — what is emerging.
narration: The earth does not whisper tonight. It screams. The fissures rip open like wounds in a body that has held them too long, and what crawls out is older than any living memory. Bone. Pale and green-lit, reassembling itself with a patience that is worse than speed. The warriors meet them with stone and fury. They fight the way their people have always fought — shoulder to shoulder, broad and unyielding. But for every bone creature that falls, two more drag themselves into the cold air. Orin drives his staff into the ground. Azra presses her palms flat against the trembling earth. They reach — together — into the dark below. What they find there makes them both gasp. This is not an attack. It is a summoning. Something is calling these bones home.

title: The Heart Below
prompt: A vertiginous downward shot into the largest fissure, where Orin and Azra descend together into a vast underground cavern illuminated by veins of green phosphorescence running through the rock walls like a circulatory system. Orin clings to a rope of twisted root and sinew, his staff strapped to his back, its amber glow pulsing like a heartbeat. Azra climbs beside him on the raw stone face, her powerful hands finding holds that no human could grip, her amber eyes wide and reflecting the green light, her face paint smeared with sweat and dust. Far below them, at the cavern's center, a massive skeletal heart — a cathedral-sized lattice of ancient bone and sinew — pulses with green fire, surrounded by the writhing forms of the bone constructs being drawn toward it. The camera is positioned above and behind the two descending figures, emphasizing the terrifying depth. The palette is deep cave black, toxic green veins, and the warm amber pulse of Orin's staff cutting through the darkness.
narrative: To stop the summoning, they must go to its source. Together they descend into the living earth. Below waits the heart of the bone entity — a pulsing lattice of ancient remains, drawing every old skeleton to itself, growing. The constructs ignore them. They are not the target. They are too alive.
narration: Down. Past root and stone and the bones of creatures that lived before the first fire was lit. Azra leads. She knows the old paths — the ones her grandmother whispered about in a voice thick with fear. Orin follows, pressing his sight forward like a lantern into fog. The green light grows thicker. Warmer. It hums against his ribs. And then the cavern opens, and they both stop breathing. Below them beats a heart made of bone. Every skeleton that ever slept in the frozen ground is being called to it — drawn like moths to a flame that should not exist. The constructs flow past them without interest. They are warm-blooded. They are alive. And being alive, down here, in this place — it means something. Azra looks at Orin. Her amber eyes say what her mouth cannot. Not yet. Not here. Wait.

title: The Seer's Gambit
prompt: A close-up action shot at the base of the bone heart, where Orin and Azra stand shoulder to shoulder on a narrow ledge of raw stone, facing the massive pulsing structure. Orin has driven his staff into the ground and both his hands grip it, veins standing out on his forearms, his eyes blazing full amber as he pushes his prophetic sight into the bone lattice — his face contorted with effort, well-defined human features, sharp nose, sweat-slick skin catching the green-amber light. Azra stands with her arms spread wide, her stone pendants shattered and scattered at her feet, the fragments glowing with deep ochre light that bleeds outward into the bone heart like ink in water. Her broad face is set with absolute determination, brow furrowed, teeth bared. The camera is at eye level, tight on both figures, with the towering bone heart looming behind them in a radial composition. The palette is the collision of amber and green, creating a volatile yellow-white at the center where their magics meet.
narrative: Azra sacrifices her pendants — generations of carved ancestral power — to crack the bone heart's shell. Orin pours his entire prophetic sight into the breach, showing the entity a future it cannot survive. Together they fracture the summoning from within. The cost is visible on both their faces.
narration: Azra breaks her pendants. One by one, by her own hands, she destroys what her grandmother built and her grandmother before that. Each shattering sounds like a rib snapping. The fragments bleed ochre light into the
